## The Emperor's New Thermostat
### *An essay by Dr. Elias Thorn*

For forty years I have studied the atmosphere, and I will say plainly what a great many of my colleagues will only say in private: the case for a climate catastrophe driven primarily by human CO₂ emissions is not settled science. It is a hypothesis that has failed its most important empirical tests, propped up by models that cannot reproduce the past and have not predicted the present. The public has been told a story of certainty that the data does not support.

My thesis is this. Two bodies of evidence—one from the satellite era, one from the deep past—converge on the same conclusion: the climate's sensitivity to CO₂ has been dramatically overstated, and the natural forces that have always driven Earth's climate are still driving it now. The anthropogenic signal is real but modest. The alarm is manufactured.

**Begin with the satellites.** Since 1979 we have had the most complete atmospheric temperature record in human history, produced by NASA-supported instruments and analyzed independently by NOAA and by John Christy and Roy Spencer at the University of Alabama in Huntsville. That record is the cleanest test we will ever have of the climate models, because the models make their sharpest predictions about the troposphere—especially the tropical mid-troposphere, where water-vapor feedback is supposed to amplify CO₂ warming into something dangerous.

The models failed that test. Christy's comparisons of the CMIP5 ensemble against satellite and independent balloon data show model-projected warming running two to three times hotter than observation across the tropical troposphere. This is not cherry-picking; it is the central tendency of the ensemble diverging from reality for four decades. And it matters enormously, because the entire catastrophe narrative depends on high climate sensitivity—on feedbacks tripling or quadrupling the roughly 1°C of direct warming that doubled CO₂ would produce on its own. The place those feedbacks should be loudest is precisely the place they are quietest. Independent energy-budget analyses by Lewis and Curry, built from the IPCC's own forcing data, arrive at sensitivity estimates well below the model mean. Two methods, one answer: the models run too hot.

**Now turn to the ice.** The Vostok and EPICA ice cores from Antarctica, together with Greenland records, give us hundreds of thousands of years of paired temperature and CO₂ data. The finding, confirmed across multiple analyses, is that across the glacial-interglacial cycles, temperature changes *precede* CO₂ changes by roughly 600 to 800 years. The Earth warms first; CO₂ rises afterward.

Researchers including Willie Soon and Nir Shaviv have emphasized what this record makes unavoidable: the dominant rhythms of Earth's climate are set by astronomical and solar forcing, not by CO₂. Orbital cycles pace the ice ages. Solar variability, amplified through mechanisms including cosmic-ray modulation of cloud cover proposed by Svensmark and pursued by Shaviv, correlates with climate shifts across geological time in ways CO₂ simply does not. The Medieval Warm Period, the Little Ice Age, the Roman Climatic Optimum—none of these required industrial emissions, and the mainstream narrative has spent decades trying to erase or flatten them precisely because their existence is so inconvenient.

The standard response is that CO₂ acts as an "amplifier" even when it lags. Perhaps. But an amplifier cannot be the primary driver of a system it enters late. The ice cores show a climate where the sun and the oceans lead, and CO₂ follows. To then turn around and insist that in our own era CO₂ has seized the wheel while solar and oceanic variability have become negligible is not a conclusion drawn from the data; it is an assumption imposed upon it.

**Consider what this combined picture implies.** The paleoclimate record tells us the system has always been governed primarily by forces we do not control. The satellite record tells us the models that claim otherwise cannot reproduce the only four decades we can actually measure with precision. And yet on the basis of those same models, entire economies are being restructured, energy poverty is being inflicted on the developing world, and scientific dissent is treated as a form of moral failure.

The failed specific predictions ought to have chastened the field long ago. An ice-free Arctic by 2013. British children who would never see snow. Tens of millions of climate refugees by 2010. Accelerating hurricane frequency. Each forecast was made with the same confidence now attached to 2050 and 2100. Each quietly expired. A discipline that cannot acknowledge its errors is not a discipline in good health.

None of this requires denying that CO₂ is a greenhouse gas or that humans have some influence on climate. The radiative physics of CO₂ is textbook; a doubling produces roughly 1°C of direct warming, all else equal. The entire argument has always been about the feedbacks—the multipliers that turn a manageable 1°C into a civilizational threat. The ice cores suggest those feedbacks, over deep time, are not what the models assume. The satellites suggest that in our own time, they are not what the models assume. Two independent witnesses tell the same story. The modelers tell another.

The climate is changing, as it has always changed. The sun varies. The oceans breathe on multi-decadal timescales we are only beginning to characterize. Orbital geometry grinds on. Against these forces, human CO₂ is a contributor, not a conductor. The honest scientific position is that we do not know the climate's sensitivity to CO₂ with anything like the precision required to justify the policies being built upon it—and the evidence we do have points consistently toward the low end of the range.

The atmosphere is the referee. The ice is the historian. For forty years one has been rendering a verdict quieter than the one we were promised, and for four hundred thousand years the other has been telling us who really runs this planet. It is past time we listened to both.

**The Case for Humility: Why I Remain Unconvinced**
*by Dr. Elias Thorn*

I have spent forty-three years studying the atmosphere. I have drilled ice, calibrated radiosondes, argued with modelers, and watched a field I love become something I barely recognize — a field where dissent is treated as heresy and where the word "settled" is applied to questions that working scientists know are anything but. I am not here to tell you that carbon dioxide does nothing. It is a greenhouse gas; the radiative physics is sound; I taught it for twenty years. I am here to tell you that the confident leap from "CO₂ warms the planet" to "CO₂ is the master dial of the climate system and we face catastrophe" is a leap the evidence does not support. It is a leap of institutional momentum, not of physics.

Let me begin with the ice cores, because they are what first made me a skeptic of the dominant narrative.

The Vostok and EPICA Dome C records, and the Greenland cores alongside them, show something that anyone can verify: across the glacial-interglacial transitions of the last 800,000 years, temperature moves first, and CO₂ follows, typically by several centuries — on the order of 800 years at the terminations, with considerable uncertainty. Now, I want to be honest with you, because honesty is the only currency I have left at my age. The mainstream interpretation of this lag is not absurd. The argument is that orbital forcing — Milankovitch cycles — initiates warming, which then releases CO₂ from the oceans, which then amplifies the warming as a feedback. On that account, CO₂ is both effect and cause, and the lag is not a refutation.

But here is what I want you to sit with. That interpretation is a *model-dependent inference*, not a direct observation. What the ice actually shows is that in every natural climate transition we have a detailed record of, temperature led and CO₂ trailed. The feedback story is plausible, but it requires CO₂ to do enormous amplification work — work whose magnitude is precisely the "climate sensitivity" question that remains, after fifty years of intensive study, unresolved to within a factor of two or three. The IPCC's own sensitivity range has barely narrowed since the Charney Report of 1979. Think about that. Half a century. Billions of dollars. Supercomputers that would have seemed like science fiction to my graduate advisors. And the central parameter of the entire edifice is still a range wide enough to drive a truck through. That is not the signature of a settled science. That is the signature of a system we do not yet understand.

So when I look at the modern record and am told that a roughly 1°C rise over 150 years, against a Holocene backdrop that includes the Medieval Warm Period and the Little Ice Age — swings of comparable magnitude driven by *no one knows quite what* — is unambiguously anthropogenic, my response is: on what basis is the ambiguity resolved? The honest answer is that it is resolved by models whose sensitivity parameter is tuned, whose cloud physics is parameterized rather than derived, and whose hindcasts match the 20th century in part because they are constructed to.

Consider solar variability. The community decided, largely in the 1990s, that total solar irradiance varies too little over the satellite era to explain modern warming, and moved on. But total irradiance is not the only channel. Svensmark and others have pursued the hypothesis that solar modulation of galactic cosmic rays affects cloud nucleation — and the CLOUD experiment at CERN has demonstrated that the ion-induced nucleation mechanism is real, even if its climatic magnitude remains contested. "Contested" is not "refuted." Yet in the public telling, solar influence has been dismissed as if the question were closed. It is not closed.

Consider the ocean. The Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation, the ENSO system — these are modes of internal variability that redistribute heat on timescales of decades to centuries. We have perhaps 150 years of instrumental record and perhaps 40 years of good satellite coverage to characterize oscillations whose periods rival or exceed our observation window. To confidently partition 20th-century warming between anthropogenic forcing and internal variability under those conditions requires an epistemic confidence I simply do not possess.

Consider the models. I have colleagues who build them; they are serious people. But the CMIP ensembles have, on the whole, run warm against observations in the tropical mid-troposphere. The discrepancy is not nothing, and it is not fully resolved by reprocessing — though I will tell you that reprocessing has narrowed it, and anyone who says the "hot spot" is simply absent is overstating their case. What I will say is that a model ensemble that systematically biases warm in a key region, and that relies on tuned aerosol forcing to match 20th-century observations, is not a tool I would bet civilizational resources on.

And this is my real objection — not to the physics of CO₂, but to the sociology of certainty that has grown up around it. I have watched grant applications rewritten to foreground climate relevance. I have watched colleagues decline to publish null results. I have watched a public discourse in which every hurricane, every wildfire, every warm winter is attributed, on television, to a cause whose quantitative fingerprint on those specific events is genuinely uncertain. I have watched the word "denier" — a word with a specific and terrible historical weight — deployed against scientists asking the kinds of questions science is supposed to welcome.

My thesis is simple, and I will not dress it up. The climate is changing, as it always has. Human beings are contributing, through CO₂ and through land-use change and through aerosols whose cooling effect partially masks whatever warming we cause. But the claim that we are the dominant driver, that the change is catastrophic, and that the science is settled — these are three separate claims, and the confidence of the third has been illegitimately transferred to the first two. Natural variability, on every timescale we can examine, has been larger and stranger than our models admit. Humility before that fact is not denial. It is science.

I am an old man, and I will not live to see which of us was right. But I will tell you what I tell my grandchildren: be suspicious of any field in which the cost of doubt is higher than the cost of conformity. That is not the field I entered in 1981. I hope it becomes that field again.
